summary: Provision new Amazon MQ brokers — ActiveMQ or RabbitMQ. Greenfield today (no broker exists yet in this estate) — the create stands up the broker and its first user; additional users, LDAP auth, and a custom engine configuration are engineer follow-ups.

operations.title: Provision a new Amazon MQ broker
operations.summary: Request a brand-new ActiveMQ or RabbitMQ message broker; an engineer finalizes and reviews the Terraform before it applies.
operations.description: Stand up a broker as a complete specification: engine, sizing, network placement, and its first user. Submitting routes it to an engineer with a draft Terraform block to review and finish — nothing is applied without review.

params.name: broker_name
params.label: Broker name
params.help: Names the broker in the Amazon MQ console and the generated draft.

params.name: engine_type
params.label: Engine
params.help: The message broker engine. Determines which deployment modes and instance types are valid.

params.name: engine_version
params.label: Engine version
params.help: Left to the engineer at review, since a hand-picked version can go stale as AWS deprecates old broker engine releases.

params.name: host_instance_type
params.label: Broker instance type
params.help: Size class for the broker's processor and memory.

params.name: deployment_mode
params.label: Deployment mode
params.help: ACTIVE_STANDBY_MULTI_AZ is ActiveMQ-only; CLUSTER_MULTI_AZ is RabbitMQ-only — confirmed against the engine at review.

params.name: storage_type
params.label: Storage type
params.help: EFS is only valid for a single-instance ActiveMQ broker; EBS applies to the other deployment modes.

params.name: publicly_accessible
params.label: Publicly accessible
params.help: Never assigned. This broker is internal-only; this guardrail is written into the draft so the reviewer sees it.

params.name: subnet_ids
params.label: Subnets
params.help: One subnet for SINGLE_INSTANCE, two (different AZs) for a Multi-AZ deployment mode.

params.name: security_groups
params.label: Security groups
params.help: Who can reach the broker's protocol endpoints.

params.name: user_username
params.label: First user — username
params.help: Every broker needs at least one user; more can be added as an engineer follow-up.

params.name: user_password
params.label: First user — password
params.help: Never entered here — the engineer sets this through the secrets manager during review.

params.name: user_console_access
params.label: First user — console access
params.help: Grants this user access to the broker's web console.

params.name: auto_minor_version_upgrade
params.label: Auto minor version upgrade
params.help: Applies minor engine version upgrades automatically during the maintenance window.

params.name: apply_immediately
params.label: Apply changes immediately
params.help: Whether future changes to this broker apply right away instead of during its maintenance window.

params.name: encryption_kms_key_id
params.label: Encryption key
params.help: Leave blank for an AWS-owned key.

params.name: resource_name
params.label: Name tag
params.help: Shown as the name everywhere — consoles, tickets, this portal.

params.name: description_tag
params.label: What this broker is for
params.help: One line an operator can act on at 3 AM.

params.name: pic
params.label: Person in charge
params.help: Who to call about this broker.

decisions: Additional broker users beyond the one authored here (the user block, a set) are an engineer follow-up.
LDAP authentication, a custom broker engine configuration (aws_mq_configuration), and cross-region data replication are engineer follow-ups after creation.
